summary: Gruber offers detailed observations on Apple's October 2005 product announcements: Aperture, updated Power Macs, and new PowerBooks. He praises Aperture's UI design and photographer-focused approach while noting it's not a Photoshop competitor but rather targets a specific segment. The Power Mac updates get credit for serious performance gains via dual-core processors, though he notes Apple is further from the promised 3 GHz than ever. The PowerBook updates are mostly display improvements with little performance gain, raising questions about the Osborne Effect as Intel transition looms.
key_points: Aperture represents Apple targeting a specific Photoshop market segment (photographers) rather than competing with Photoshop directly as a design tool
Aperture's single-window UI philosophy versus Photoshop's palette-heavy approach signals the future direction of Mac application design
The new dual-core Power Macs deliver major real-world performance gains (76% faster Xcode builds) but clock speeds have actually regressed from 2.7 to 2.5 GHz
PowerBook updates are mostly display improvements, making them a real test of the Osborne Effect as customers weigh buying now versus waiting for Intel-based models
Apple's online store is experimenting with Ajax-based configuration UI that replaces pop-up menus, though it's inconsistently deployed
money_quotes: Aperture looks like what Mac apps are going to look like in the future; Photoshop looks like what Mac apps used to look like years ago.
If Aperture is fast, then why is iPhoto still so slow?
Not only have the Power Mac G5s still not gotten to 3 GHz, they're actually further away than before, topping out at 2.5.
Finally, Power Mac owners with thousands of dollars budgeted for RAM can comfortably open both Safari and iPhoto at the same time.
I think well-designed single-main-window designs are a better way to implement 'workflow' applications.
If you're tempted to wait for Intel-based PowerBooks, at this point, I think you might be waiting for a long time.
Aperture's revision control only stores the instructions, not entire files for each version.
If you've ever used Xcode, or listened to a developer who does, you'll realize that there are a lot of Mac developers out there clicking 'buy' today.
key_insight: Apple's October 2005 updates reveal a company pushing UI design forward with Aperture while squeezing the last viable performance from PowerPC hardware as the Intel transition approaches.
